title: The Satavahanas
intro: Let's learn about the Satavahanas — the first great dynasty of the Deccan (c. 2nd century BCE to 3rd century CE), whose earliest coins and capital sites lie in today's Telangana.
explanation:
- **Homeland**: early sites like **Kotalingala** (Jagtial district, on the Godavari) yielded coins of pre-Satavahana and early Satavahana rulers such as **Simuka**, the founder. Later capitals included **Pratishthana (Paithan)** and **Dharanikota/Amaravati**.
- **Greatest ruler**: **Gautamiputra Satakarni** (c. 2nd century CE) — defeated the Saka ruler Nahapana; his mother **Gautami Balasri's Nashik inscription** praises him as 'destroyer of Sakas, Yavanas and Pahlavas'.
- **Administration**: kingdom divided into *aharas*; officials called *amatyas*; land grants to Brahmins and Buddhist monks began under them.
- **Economy**: cotton textiles, inland and overseas **trade with Rome**; lead, copper and potin **coins**; ports like Kalyan and inland towns flourished.
- **Culture**: king **Hala** composed **Gathasaptasati** in Prakrit; patronage to Buddhism — **Amaravati stupa** art reached its peak under them.
example: The **Kotalingala excavations** in Telangana produced coins of Simuka — evidence used to argue that the Satavahanas rose first from the Telangana region, not Maharashtra. TGPSC loves this Telangana-first angle!

test.q: Who was the founder of the Satavahana dynasty?
test.why: Simuka founded the dynasty; his coins were found at Kotalingala.

test.q: Kotalingala, an early Satavahana site, is located on which river?
test.why: Kotalingala lies on the Godavari in Jagtial district.

test.q: Gathasaptasati was composed by:
test.why: King Hala compiled this Prakrit anthology of 700 verses.

test.q: The Nashik inscription praising Gautamiputra Satakarni was issued by:
test.why: His mother Gautami Balasri recorded his achievements at Nashik.

test.q: Satavahana coins were mostly made of:
test.why: They issued mainly lead, potin and copper coins; gold coinage was rare.

title: Ikshvakus and Vishnukundins
intro: After the Satavahanas, two dynasties kept Telangana–Andhra region prominent: the Ikshvakus of Nagarjunakonda and the Vishnukundins.
explanation:
- **Ikshvakus (c. 3rd century CE)**: capital **Vijayapuri (Nagarjunakonda)** on the Krishna. Founder **Vasishthiputra Chamtamula**. Royal women like **Chamtisiri** built Buddhist stupas and viharas — Nagarjunakonda became a great **Mahayana Buddhist centre** associated with Acharya **Nagarjuna**.
- Kings performed Vedic sacrifices while queens patronised Buddhism — a famous example of religious co-existence.
- **Vishnukundins (c. 5th–7th century CE)**: ruled from sites like **Keesaragutta (near Hyderabad)** and Amarapura; important rulers **Madhava Varma II** (greatest, performed many sacrifices) and **Vikramendra Varma**. They patronised the **Undavalli caves**-style rock architecture and issued coins with lion motifs.
- These dynasties bridge the gap between the Satavahanas and the Chalukyas in Deccan history.
example: When **Nagarjunasagar dam** was built, the Buddhist monuments of Nagarjunakonda were carefully shifted to a hilltop island museum — today you can see Ikshvaku-era stupas rescued from submergence, a favourite culture question.

test.q: The capital of the Ikshvakus was:
test.why: Vijayapuri on the Krishna, modern Nagarjunakonda, was their capital.

test.q: Founder of the Ikshvaku dynasty was:
test.why: Chamtamula founded the line; his sister/daughters-in-law built Buddhist monuments.

test.q: Keesaragutta, a Vishnukundin site, is near:
test.why: Keesaragutta lies near Hyderabad and shows Vishnukundin temple remains.

test.q: The greatest Vishnukundin ruler, famous for Vedic sacrifices, was:
test.why: Madhava Varma II performed numerous asvamedha and other sacrifices.

test.q: Nagarjunakonda was a great centre of:
test.why: It flourished as a Mahayana centre linked to Acharya Nagarjuna.

title: The Kakatiyas of Warangal
intro: Let's learn about the Kakatiyas (c. 1000–1323 CE) — the dynasty that gave Telangana its golden age, its tanks, and its identity symbols.
explanation:
- **Capitals**: first **Hanamkonda**, then **Orugallu (Warangal)** with its famous stone fort.
- **Key rulers**: **Ganapati Deva** (greatest expansionist; issued the **Motupalli Abhaya Sasanam** assuring safety to foreign traders), **Rani Rudrama Devi** (one of India's few women rulers; Marco Polo praised her rule), and **Prataparudra II** (last ruler; captured by the Delhi Sultanate in **1323**).
- **Irrigation**: built chains of **tanks** — Ramappa, Pakhal, Laknavaram — the basis of Telangana agriculture even today (inspiration for Mission Kakatiya).
- **Architecture**: **Thousand Pillar Temple** (Hanamkonda), **Ramappa (Rudresvara) Temple** — a **UNESCO World Heritage Site (2021)** with floating bricks; **Kakatiya Thoranam** (Warangal gateway) is on the Telangana state emblem.
- Administration used the **Nayankara system** (military chiefs granted land).
example: The **Kakatiya Thoranam** appears in the official emblem of Telangana state, and **Mission Kakatiya** (tank restoration programme) is named after the dynasty — history directly feeding modern policy and exam questions.

test.q: The Motupalli Abhaya Sasanam (charter of safety to sea traders) was issued by:
test.why: Ganapati Deva issued it at the port of Motupalli to encourage foreign trade.

test.q: Which traveller praised Rudrama Devi's rule?
test.why: Marco Polo visited the Kakatiya kingdom and admired her administration.

test.q: Ramappa Temple became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in:
test.why: The Rudresvara (Ramappa) temple was inscribed in 2021.

test.q: The last Kakatiya ruler, defeated in 1323, was:
test.why: Prataparudra II was captured by Ulugh Khan's Delhi forces in 1323.

test.q: Mission Kakatiya, named after the dynasty, aims at:
test.why: It restores the minor irrigation tanks originally built in the Kakatiya era.

title: The Qutb Shahis and the Founding of Hyderabad
intro: Let's learn about the Qutb Shahi dynasty (1518–1687) — builders of Golconda and Hyderabad, and patrons of a composite Telugu–Persian culture.
explanation:
- **Founder**: **Sultan Quli Qutb-ul-Mulk**, a governor of the Bahmani kingdom, declared independence at **Golconda in 1518**.
- **Golconda fort** became the capital — famous for its acoustics, water system and the **diamond trade** (Koh-i-Noor and Hope diamonds passed through Golconda's markets).
- **Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah** founded **Hyderabad in 1591** on the Musi river and built the **Charminar** (commemorating the end of an epidemic) and Mecca Masjid was begun under the dynasty.
- **Culture**: patronised **Telugu** alongside Persian/Deccani Urdu — Muhammad Quli himself wrote poetry; the court hosted poets like **Kshetrayya**'s tradition; Ibrahim Qutb Shah ('Malkibharama' to Telugu poets) was famous for Telugu patronage.
- **End**: **Aurangzeb captured Golconda in 1687** after a long siege (betrayal at the gates), ending the dynasty; the last ruler **Abul Hasan Tana Shah** died a prisoner. His general **Ramadasu (Kancherla Gopanna)**, the Bhadrachalam devotee-poet, is part of this era's lore.
example: **Charminar (1591)** was built where the city was founded, at the crossing of roads to key trade centres — an example of a monument answering 'when, why and by whom' in one structure: Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah, plague's end, new capital Hyderabad.

test.q: The Qutb Shahi dynasty was founded in 1518 by:
test.why: Sultan Quli, a Bahmani governor, became independent at Golconda.

test.q: Hyderabad city was founded in 1591 by:
test.why: Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah founded Hyderabad and built the Charminar.

test.q: Golconda was captured by Aurangzeb in:
test.why: After a months-long siege, Golconda fell in 1687.

test.q: Which Qutb Shahi ruler was affectionately called 'Malkibharama' by Telugu poets?
test.why: Ibrahim Qutb Shah generously patronised Telugu literature.

test.q: The devotee-poet Ramadasu, associated with Bhadrachalam temple, lived under:
test.why: Kancherla Gopanna (Ramadasu) served as a tahsildar under Abul Hasan Tana Shah.

title: The Asaf Jahis (Nizams of Hyderabad)
intro: Let's learn about the Asaf Jahi dynasty (1724–1948) — the Nizams who ruled Hyderabad State, of which Telangana was the core.
explanation:
- **Founder**: **Mir Qamruddin (Nizam-ul-Mulk, Asaf Jah I)** defeated Mubariz Khan at the **Battle of Shakar Kheda (1724)** and became independent ruler of the Deccan.
- Hyderabad became the largest princely state; the Nizam accepted the **Subsidiary Alliance** with the British (1798, first Indian state to do so).
- **Salar Jung I** (Diwan, 1853–83): modernised administration — new departments, railways, and reforms that shaped the state.
- **Mir Osman Ali Khan (7th Nizam, 1911–48)**: founded **Osmania University (1918** — first Indian university with a vernacular medium, Urdu), built the **High Court, Osmania Hospital**, and the **Nizamsagar** project; once called the richest man in the world.
- Social awakening: **Andhra Mahasabha**, library movement (**Sri Krishna Devaraya Andhra Bhasha Nilayam, 1901**), and Arya Samaj activities challenged feudal rule, leading to the 1940s upheavals.
example: **Osmania University (1918)** teaching in Urdu medium was a landmark: it later became the intellectual centre of both the 1969 Jai Telangana agitation and the 2009–14 statehood movement — one campus linking three eras of Telangana history.

test.q: The Asaf Jahi dynasty was founded after which battle in 1724?
test.why: Nizam-ul-Mulk defeated Mubariz Khan at Shakar Kheda and took the Deccan.

test.q: The first Indian state to join the British Subsidiary Alliance was:
test.why: The Nizam signed the subsidiary alliance with Wellesley in 1798.

test.q: Osmania University was founded in:
test.why: The 7th Nizam founded it in 1918 with Urdu as the medium.

test.q: The great reformer Diwan of Hyderabad (1853–83) was:
test.why: Salar Jung I modernised Hyderabad's administration.

test.q: Sri Krishna Devaraya Andhra Bhasha Nilayam (1901) is associated with:
test.why: It sparked the library movement and Telugu cultural awakening in the Nizam's state.

title: Telangana Armed Struggle and Operation Polo (1946–48)
intro: Let's learn about the peasant armed struggle against the Nizam's feudal system, and how Hyderabad State merged into India in 1948.
explanation:
- **Background**: peasants suffered under *vetti* (forced labour), illegal exactions by **jagirdars/deshmukhs**, and the Nizam's levies. The **Andhra Mahasabha** and Communists organised them.
- **Spark (1946)**: **Doddi Komarayya** was killed at Kadivendi village (Jangaon) — his martyrdom ignited the armed revolt. **Chakali Ilamma** of Palakurthi famously defended her land against the deshmukh — an icon of the struggle.
- Villages formed **gram rajyams** (village communes), redistributed land, and fought the Nizam's police and the **Razakars** — the private militia led by **Kasim Razvi** of the MIM, which committed widespread atrocities.
- **Operation Polo (13–17 September 1948)**: the Indian Army's 'police action' under the leadership of Sardar Patel's ministry; Hyderabad surrendered on **17 September 1948** and merged with India.
- The Communists continued the struggle till **1951**; the movement forced land reforms like the abolition of jagirdari.
example: **Chakali Ilamma's** resistance — a washerwoman refusing to give up her 4 acres to the Visnoor deshmukh — became the symbol of the struggle; Telangana still honours her, and 17 September is officially commemorated as the day Hyderabad joined India.

test.q: Whose martyrdom at Kadivendi (1946) began the Telangana armed struggle?
test.why: Doddi Komarayya was shot by the deshmukh's men at Kadivendi, Jangaon.

test.q: 'Vetti' in the Nizam's Telangana meant:
test.why: Vetti was unpaid forced labour extracted from lower castes by landlords.

test.q: The Razakar militia was led by:
test.why: Kasim Razvi of the MIM led the Razakars' violent campaign.

test.q: Operation Polo concluded with Hyderabad's surrender on:
test.why: The five-day police action ended on 17 September 1948.

test.q: Chakali Ilamma is remembered for:
options: Defending her land against the deshmukh
test.why: Her defiance of the Visnoor deshmukh made her an icon of the peasant struggle.

title: Mulki Movement, Gentlemen's Agreement and the 1969 Agitation
intro: Let's learn how Telangana's grievances inside united Andhra Pradesh grew — from Mulki rules to the massive 1969 'Jai Telangana' agitation.
explanation:
- **Mulki rules**: Nizam-era rules (from 1919) reserving government jobs for locals (*mulkis*). The **1952 Mulki agitation** in Hyderabad city protested outsiders taking jobs.
- **States Reorganisation (1956)**: despite the SRC (Fazal Ali Commission) suggesting Telangana remain separate for some years ('Telangana could unite later if its assembly agreed'), **Andhra Pradesh was formed on 1 November 1956** after the **Gentlemen's Agreement** — promises on jobs, budget surpluses, education and a Regional Council for Telangana.
- **Broken promises**: Telangana's budget surpluses were diverted, Mulki safeguards were violated, irrigation projects favoured coastal Andhra.
- **1969 Agitation**: began at Khammam/Palvancha with students; **369 people** (mostly students) died in police firing. **Dr. Marri Chenna Reddy** formed the **Telangana Praja Samithi (TPS)**, which won 10 of 14 Lok Sabha seats in 1971 — but merged with Congress after the **8-point/5-point formulas** and the Supreme Court's upholding of Mulki rules (1972); a separate state was denied.
example: The **Gentlemen's Agreement (20 February 1956)** — signed by leaders like B. Gopala Reddy and B. Rama Krishna Rao — is the classic exam example of a political safeguard that failed in practice, directly fuelling both the 1969 and the 2001–14 movements.

test.q: The Gentlemen's Agreement preceding AP's formation was signed in:
test.why: It was signed on 20 February 1956; AP formed on 1 November 1956.

test.q: The SRC (1955) that cautioned against immediate merger was headed by:
test.why: The Fazal Ali Commission recommended Telangana stay separate initially.

test.q: How many people died in the 1969 Telangana agitation?
test.why: Police firing killed around 369 people, mostly students.

test.q: The Telangana Praja Samithi (1969) was founded by:
test.why: Dr. Marri Chenna Reddy led the TPS, which swept the 1971 polls in Telangana.

test.q: 'Mulki' rules were about:
test.why: Mulki rules reserved public employment for residents of Hyderabad State.

title: The Statehood Movement (1996–2014) and Formation of Telangana
intro: Let's learn the final phase — how a decades-old dream became India's 29th state on 2 June 2014.
explanation:
- **Revival (1990s)**: intellectual groundwork by **Prof. K. Jayashankar** ('ideologue of Telangana'); organisations and public meetings revived the demand after 1969's failure.
- **TRS founded (27 April 2001)** by **K. Chandrashekar Rao (KCR)** at Jala Drushyam, with separate statehood as its single agenda.
- **2009**: KCR's **fast-unto-death** (from 29 November) forced the Centre's **9 December 2009 statement** (P. Chidambaram) that the process of forming Telangana would begin — then put on hold after Seemandhra protests, triggering mass resignations and the **Million March, Sakala Janula Samme (42-day general strike, 2011)**, and students' sacrifices.
- **Sri Krishna Committee (2010)** examined options; ultimately the **AP Reorganisation Act, 2014** passed Parliament in February 2014.
- **2 June 2014**: Telangana became the **29th state**; **Hyderabad** as common capital for up to 10 years; KCR became the first Chief Minister. (**Formation Day: 2 June**.)
example: **Sakala Janula Samme (2011)** — a 42-day strike where government employees, teachers, singers and even Singareni coal miners stopped work — is the textbook example of a peaceful mass movement that made statehood unstoppable.

test.q: TRS (now BRS) was founded on 27 April 2001 by:
test.why: KCR founded the Telangana Rashtra Samithi in 2001.

test.q: The Centre's famous statement promising initiation of Telangana's formation came on:
test.why: Home Minister Chidambaram's 9 December 2009 statement followed KCR's fast.

test.q: The Sri Krishna Committee (2010) was set up to:
test.why: It studied the situation in AP and gave six options, including statehood.

test.q: The 42-day general strike of 2011 was called:
test.why: The 'strike of all people' paralysed the region for 42 days in late 2011.

test.q: Telangana was formed on:
test.why: Appointed day under the AP Reorganisation Act was 2 June 2014.
